name: Cova de can Bordils
name_english: Cave of Can Bordils (also Cave of the Hiding Places)
starting_point: Ma-4023 road km 2.400 between Porto Cristo and Cala Millor. After passing the second right turn toward Cala Millor, at 177 m you'll find the gate to the property. Ring the bell for the owner to let you in.
best_season: Any, although swimming season is recommended
equipment: No vertical progression equipment needed. Only helmet and lighting.
description: Located on private farmland just 70 m from the house, this cave is accessed via steps carved into the ground beneath a fig tree. The cave descends to approximately sea level and contains a gallery with a small lake near the entrance, a main chamber with ceiling formations, and a crystal-clear saltwater lake. The cave has three lakes of varying sizes. It is one of 30 ZEC-designated caves on Mallorca, requiring official permission to visit.
full_description:
It is surprising the relationship that fig trees have with the entrances of cavities on the coast of Manacor. Once again, upon making the first contact with the Cova de can Bordils or dets Amagatalls, the first thing I could see as soon as I approached is that a beautiful fig tree protrudes from the interior of the cave, just like some others in the area, such as its neighbors the Cova de sa Figuera and the Gruta de l'Amo den Martí, which are very close by. Perhaps one day this matter will need to be analyzed, because although one always tends to think — and it may indeed be true — that bird droppings can contain seeds that subsequently germinate, why is it always fig tree seeds that end up germinating and not others?

The Cova de can Bordils or dets Amagatalls is located right on the Can Bordils estate, about seventy meters from the house, so it is necessary to ask permission to visit from Sr. Pedro, who will kindly allow it (at least during our visit he did not put up the slightest objection and everything was made easy for us).

To access the cave, we descend via stairs carved into the ground that are usually quite dirty, both from branches pruned from the fig tree itself growing next to the stairs, and from the garbage that the property owners throw into the interior of the cave, not to mention dead animals — sheep, goats, chickens, etc. In fact, in this first part of the cave there is a large quantity of bones that, depending on the person, can make quite an impression.

Navigating around the garbage, we continue descending progressively until we reach the floor of the cave, which must be at approximately zero meters altitude, where on our left we can observe a gallery and at its entrance a small lake that seems to block the way but can be circumvented. On the first visit that Mallorca Verde made to the cavity, we could not visit this entire gallery due to lack of time, as the owners had to leave, and we left it pending for another day. So we continued straight ahead through a wide corridor that places us in a sort of esplanade in a chamber with some formations, mainly on certain areas of the ceiling of the cavity as well as on the left side. On our right and several meters below us lies a lake of crystal-clear saltwater.

Once outside the cave and talking with the property owner, he told us that once you cross to the other side of the first lake we found going down on our left, the gallery continues until reaching another one where it ends. So the cavity has three lakes of different dimensions, with the last one remaining for us to explore on the next visit. Once the lake that had been pending from the previous visit has been visited, this Web page remains pending an update of the cavity.

NOTE: The Cova de Can Bordils is one of the 30 ZEC cavities that currently exist on the island of Mallorca. For this reason, access is prohibited without the corresponding permit from the Ministry of the Environment.
